title: Fitting a plywood back panel to open-back kitchen cabinets
subtitle: Add a solid back to wall-mounted or base cabinets that have no factory back panel
excerpt: How to cut and fix a 6 mm plywood or hardboard back panel to a kitchen cabinet that has no factory-fitted back. Covers measuring the rebate or face-fixed position, cutting the panel, applying adhesive and pins, and finishing the exposed edge.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 8212: Code of practice for dry lining and partitioning. Cabinet-making practice, City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture-making. Trade kitchen installation guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stepped recess machined into the inner face of the cabinet side, top, and bottom panels, into which the back panel fits flush. A rebated back sits inside the cabinet and leaves the outer surfaces flush. Cheaper carcases may have no rebate — the back panel must be face-fixed (pinned directly to the outer face of the side, top, and bottom edges), which leaves a visible edge.

aliases: adding back to open kitchen unit

content.text: Some kitchen cabinets have no back panel. This is common with older units, secondhand kitchens, and budget carcases. Without a back, the cabinet racks on its wall bracket, moisture from the wall gets in, and items fall behind shelves. A 6 mm plywood or 3 mm hardboard back panel, glued and pinned into the
(or face-fixed to the rear edges), solves all three problems.

6 mm birch plywood or 3 mm hardboard (measure the back opening of the cabinet). PVA glue. 20 mm panel pins. A pin hammer or electric brad nailer. A jigsaw for cutting the panel. A sanding block for cleaning the cut edges. A tape measure and combination square.

Measure the width between the inner side panels and the height between the inner top and bottom panels. If the cabinet has a rebate, measure the rebate opening. If there is no rebate, cut the panel to fit between the outer faces of the side, top, and bottom panels. A face-fixed panel will be visible on all four edges.

Mark the panel dimensions on the plywood with a pencil and straight-edge, then cut with the jigsaw. On the face side (the side that will be visible inside the cabinet), apply masking tape along the jigsaw cut line before cutting; this prevents tear-out on the face veneer. Cut slowly with a fine-tooth blade. Sand the cut edges smooth with 120-grit.
Step 3, fit and fix the panel

Apply PVA glue to the rebate, or to the rear edges of the side, top, and bottom panels for a face-fixed back. Press the panel in and check it is square: equal diagonals means square. Drive 20 mm panel pins at 150 mm centres around all four edges, keeping pins 10 mm from the edge. Allow the glue to dry for 2 hours before hanging the cabinet.
Step 4, finish the exposed edge (face-fixed backs only)

On face-fixed backs, the plywood edge is visible at the cabinet opening. Apply iron-on melamine edge tape in a colour matching the cabinet interior; available in 19 mm and 22 mm widths. Press with a household iron on a medium-heat setting and trim with the utility knife. This gives a clean finish to the visible edge without the need for solid-timber lipping.

attrs.symptom: Panel buckles or bows after fitting
attrs.cause: Panel was stored or transported in a damp environment and has taken on moisture.
attrs.fix: Store plywood flat in a dry room for 24 to 48 hours before cutting and fitting. Once fixed into the cabinet with pins all around, bowing usually resolves as the panel equalises moisture content.
